issue_title: Range can never include child products
issue_body:
There is no good way to apply a discount to a child product.

Here's a scenario: You run a bookstore and want to discount hard cover books, but not paperbacks. Your catalog is set up so that paperback and hardcovers are children. You want to discount hardcovers but not paperbacks. One approach would be to create a new `Range` and add all the hard covers to the new `Range`, but ranges are always associated with parents or standalone products.

`Range.contains_product` has the following lines:

```python
# Child products are never part of the range, but the parent may be.
if product.is_child:
    product = product.parent
```

```shell
# apt-cache search dotnet-dev-1.0.0-preview2
dotnet-dev-1.0.0-preview2-003121 - Microsoft .NET Core 1.0.0 - SDK Preview 2
dotnet-dev-1.0.0-preview2-003131 - Microsoft .NET Core 1.0.1 - SDK 1.0.0 Preview 2-003131

dotnet-dev-1.0.0-preview2-1-003177 - Microsoft .NET Core 1.1.0 - SDK 1.0.0 Preview 2.1-003177
dotnet-dev-1.0.0-preview2.1-003155 - Microsoft .NET Core 1.1.0 Preview1 - SDK 1.0.0 Preview 2.1-003155

Looking at builds `003155` and `003177`, the package name formats are slightly different:

```
# preview2.1 with a period
dotnet-dev-1.0.0-preview2.1-003155

dotnet-dev-1.0.0-preview2-1-003177

I know it seems minute, it was something I noticed when I called `sort` in Bash.

issue_title: CachedSchemaRegistryClient fails on unhashable avro schemas
issue_body:
This is due to a bug in `avro` where schemas are not hashable (and therefore not able to be used as dict keys): https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AVRO-1737. Adding this issue to track upstream and so others with the same issue can easily search for a solution.

If you are presented with something like this:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
  .../site-packages/confluent_kafka/avro/cached_schema_registry_client.py", line 121, in register
    schema_id = schemas_to_id.get(avro_schema, None)
TypeError: unhashable type: 'EnumSchema'
```

You can patch the `__hash__` function for each schema type as a fix. Patching the parent `Schema` class does not work!

```python
# Monkey patch to get hashable avro schemas
# https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AVRO-1737
def hash_func(self):
    return hash(str(self))
schema.EnumSchema.__hash__ = hash_func
schema.RecordSchema.__hash__ = hash_func
schema.PrimitiveSchema.__hash__ = hash_func
schema.ArraySchema.__hash__ = hash_func
schema.FixedSchema.__hash__ = hash_func
schema.MapSchema.__hash__ = hash_fun
```

issue_title: Question: Is it possible to configure a consumer to not automatically reconnect?
issue_body:
This may seem like an odd question, but I have a need to better control when a consumer reconnects. I have an application with many consumers, and I need to ensure that they all connect and (more importantly) reconnect in a very orderly manner.  I am aware of `reconnect.backoff.jitter.ms` but this doesn't help as my consumers are in separate processes, and I need to coordinate their reconnections over time.

As I see it, there are two possibilities that would help:
- A way to configure the consumer to never reconnect automatically. I would also need to be aware of when the consumer became disconnected (throwing an error from `poll()` would be fine).
- Failing that, a way to become aware that a consumer has been disconnected, and is about to reconnect. This would allow me to manually intervene to shut down the consumer and restart it at a time that is appropriate.

After digging around the docs a little, I don't see that either of these things are possible, but I'm hoping that someone can prove me wrong 😉

issue_body:
Docstring for CopyRecord has this "like a Record-only deepcopy."  Ambiguous what a Record-only deepcopy is.  In actuality it only copies one extra level deep so any mutable nested data structures will still be shared between the original and the copy.

Looks like __deepcopy__ is implemented on RecordClass and does a true deepcopy.

Maybe just clean up the docstring of this function and refer users to deepcopy if they want a true deepcopy.

issue_title: Add support of VALUE=DATE and TZID when parsing EXDATE rule
issue_body:
According to the RFC 5545, the EXDATE grammar is:


       exdate     = "EXDATE" exdtparam ":" exdtval *("," exdtval) CRLF

       exdtparam  = *(
                  ;
                  ; The following are OPTIONAL,
                  ; but MUST NOT occur more than once.
                  ;
                  (";" "VALUE" "=" ("DATE-TIME" / "DATE")) /
                  ;
                  (";" tzidparam) /
                  ;
                  ; The following is OPTIONAL,
                  ; and MAY occur more than once.
                  ;
                  (";" other-param)
                  ;
                  )

So all the following examples are valid (let's suppose some valid RRULE preceed the exception set here):

     EXDATE:20170705T143000
     EXDATE;VALUE=DATE-TIME:20170705T143000
     EXDATE;VALUE=DATE:20170705
     EXDATE;TZID=Europe/Paris:20170705T143000
     EXDATE;VALUE=DATE-TIME;TZID=Europe/Paris:20170705T143000

Yet, dateutil only supports the two first formats. Indeed, DATE and TZID params are marked as unsupported (see `_parse_rfc` in rrule.py). Digging into the code of dateutil, I don't see any use of the `parms` list except to check list of param name given in the recurrence rule. However this change might be expanded to other properties, such as DTSTART, as discussed in https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/issues/401?

issue_title: Marker and other features are not accessible.
issue_body:
Since 9abdd8f4d8ff5d106ff3f681c9150a039b9d7872 Marker, Popup and other features are not accessible from `folium.features`.

I believe what happened is that pep8 marked the imports as unused (as those classes are not used in `folium.features`), and tests did not catch that because they still involve the legacy usage (e.g. `Map.simple_marker` instead of `Map.add_to(Marker)`).

Two things we need to do to avoid this from happening again:
- We should decide where to access features from. I propose the root namespace).
- We should add / modify tests to use also the new feature addition.

issue_title: Marshmallow swagger extension does not support basePath
issue_body:
Related to #69.

Flask allows configuring the `APPLICATION_ROOT`, which provides the base path for the routes. #70 fixed this issue so that the flask extension reports the fully qualified path.

When the specs are dumped using the swagger extension everything works, but the full path is shown on every route, even though the swagger specification supports the [`basePath`](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#fixed-fields) field on the root object.

I think it is important to keep the meaningful portion of the route URL separate from the base path to maintain readable documentation. #70 was the right fix for the flask extension since the `apispec` core assumes paths are absolute, but the swagger extension should be able to document the base path without needing to know about the flask configuration details.

The fact that both `flask` and `swagger` both support a configurable base path indicates that the `apispec` core should probably internalize the concept of base paths and expose them to extensions.

Examples of base path in similar libraries:

 - [Bottle: ResourceManager (default) path](http://bottlepy.org/docs/dev/api.html#bottle.ResourceManager)
 - [RAML: Base URI](https://github.com/raml-org/raml-spec/blob/master/versions/raml-10/raml-10.md/#base-uri-and-base-uri-parameters)

issue_title: validation optional fields
issue_body:
Our shop is taking a hard look at using Marshmallow for data validation and serialization.

One issue we've come across is how to correctly handle validating optional fields where the passed field value is None.  For Django forms, this is OK.  e.g. if field_a is optional and the passed data structure either has no key for field_a or the key for field_a points to None, the form will still validate (at least for this field).  In the case of Marshmallow, only former works.  i.e. for all optional keys currently set to None, we must removed them from the dictionary before attempting to pass to the Schema object.  This seems a bit cludgey and I wonder if there's a better way to handle this situation with Marshmallow.

a few examples below further illustrate my point.

```python
class AdamSchema(Schema):
    field_a = fields.Integer(required=False, 
                             validate=lambda n: n > 40)
    field_b = fields.Integer()

# 1
# works (i.e. throws expected validation error correctly)
schema = AdamSchema()
result, errors = schema.load({'field_a': 2, 'field_b': 12})

# 2
# passes (i.e. field_a not passed and schema validates)
schema = AdamSchema()
result, errors = schema.load({'field_b': 12})

# 3
# fails (i.e. field_a passed as None and schema doesn't validate)
schema = AdamSchema()
result, errors = schema.load({'field_a': None, 'field_b': 12})


#possible solution via @pre_load and @post_load

from marshmallow import Schema, pre_load, post_load

class AdamSchema2(Schema):
    field_a = fields.Integer(required=False, 
                             validate=lambda n: n is None or n > 40)
    field_b = fields.Integer()

    @pre_load
    def remove_nones(self, in_data):
        "get rid of fields set to None that may not validate correctly"
        self.nones = []
        for key in in_data.keys():
            if in_data[key] is None:
                self.nones.append(key)
                del in_data[key]
        return in_data
        

    @post_load
    def replace_nones(self, item):
        "put back in the None fields, so serialized_output = expected_output"
        for key in self.nones:
            item[key] = None
        return item

schema = AdamSchema2()
result, errors = schema.load({'field_a': None, 'field_b': 12})
# passes (no validation errors)
```

from marshmallow import Schema
from marshmallow import pre_load
from marshmallow import post_dump
from marshmallow.fields import Dict

class MySchema(Schema):
  props = Dict(required=False)

  @pre_load
  def pre_process(self, data):
    data['props'] = json.loads(data['props'])

  @post_dump
  def post_process(self, item):
    data['props'] = json.dumps(data['props'])
```

(in the end `props` is saved/loaded in/from a PostgreSQL JSON column)

Imagine the following use case:

1. I'm serializing an object (SQLAlchemy entity) according to the Schema’s fields (.dump(entity))
2. I'm populating a form with the serialized data (`props` is a string)
3. The form is submitted
4. I'm loading submitted data (.load(request.POST)), as I have a `@pre_load` decorator `props` is now a `dict`
5. Unfortunately validation failed, so I have to re-submit the form
6. Problem is that `post_process` is not called and that `props` is still a dict, whereas some client-side code expect a string

Is there a proper "Marshmallow-way" to handle this, or should I just create a custom field ?

Thanks!

(BTW is there an "official" place to ask questions like this?)

issue_title: Empty Only Treated as None
issue_body:
When the `only` parameter is an empty list/set, it causes all the fields to be de/serialized like `None`. The implementation of 2.x and 3.0 are not in compliance with their respective docs.

> **only** (_tuple_) – A list or tuple of fields to serialize. If **None**, all fields will be serialized.

```py
class TestSchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Field()
sch = TestSchema(only=())
data = dict(foo='bar')
result = sch.dump(data)
assert 'foo' not in result

# assert 'foo' not in {'foo': 'bar'}
```

This could create a security vulnerability if an application was dynamically generating the field set based on security role. A filter that was meant to hide everything would inadvertently show everything.

issue_title: Poor validator inheritance
issue_body:
The inheritance model for [`Range`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/validate.py#L159) and its subclass [`Length`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/validate.py#L201) does not seem semantically valid. This issue arose when [attempting to differentiate the two](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/pull/73) when duck typing the validators.

`Length` is not really a type of `Range`. `Range` validates numbers, and `Length` validates sequences and collections. This indicates that their domains are disjoint.

It would be more appropriate for the `Length` validator to have an attribute named something like `lengthRange` which is an instance of the `Range` validator.

This issue is equivalent to extending an `Engine` class to create a `Car` class. A `Car` has an `Engine`, but it is not a type of `Engine` itself.

issue_title: Semantics of Z3_OP_FPA_MIN_I and Z3_OP_FPA_MAX_I plus simplifier bug?
issue_body:
This similar to #1132 . It seems Z3's simplifier is leaking more undocumented operators.

e.g. 

If we start with this

```
; Start decls (3)
(declare-fun r () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun y () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
; End decls
; Start constraints (4)
(assert (= x (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (= y (fp #b1 #b00101101000 #x422c9af77f44c)))
(assert (= r (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (= (fp.max x y) r))
; End constraints
```

and then run Z3's simplifier we get

```
; Start decls (3)
(declare-fun r () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun y () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
; End decls
; Start constraints (4)
(assert (= x (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (= y (fp #b1 #b00101101000 #x422c9af77f44c)))
(assert (= r (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (let ((a!1 (and (fp.isZero x)
                (fp.isZero y)
                (or (and (fp.isPositive x) (fp.isNegative y))
                    (and (fp.isNegative x) (fp.isPositive y))))))
  (= (ite a!1 (fp.max_unspecified x y) (fp.max_i x y)) r)))
; End constraints
```
`fp.max_i` appears to correspond to `Z3_OP_FPA_MAX_I` kind and `fp.max_unspecified` corresponds publicly to `Z3_OP_UNINTERPRETED` (internally `OP_FPA_INTERNAL_MAX_UNSPECIFIED`)

It would nice if `Z3_OP_FPA_MAX_I` and `Z3_OP_FPA_MIN_I` were documented. It looks like they are introduced by `fpa_rewriter::mk_max()` and `fpa_rewriter::mk_min()`. Reading the implementation I'm guessing the semantics of `Z3_OP_FPA_MAX_I` can be assumed to be that the arguments are not `(+zero)` and `(-zero)` (similar for` Z3_OP_FPA_MAX_I`).

It would be nice if `OP_FPA_INTERNAL_MAX_UNSPECIFIED` (and the others see `Z3_get_decl_kind()` implementation) had their own public separate kinds because I need to handle each of these specially if the simplifier is going to introduce them. `Z3_OP_UNINTERPRETED` isn't enough information for my use case. I could get the string value of the AST but that's kind of a hack.

On a related noted to me it seems like there's a bug (or missing feature) when the `propagate-values` and `simplifer` tactics are combined.

```
; Start decls (3)
(declare-fun r () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun y () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
; End decls
; Start constraints (4)
(assert (= x (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (= y (fp #b1 #b00101101000 #x422c9af77f44c)))
(assert (= r (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (= (fp.max x y) r))
; End constraints
(apply (and-then propagate-values simplify) :print_benchmark true :print false)
```

The result is

```
(declare-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun y () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun r () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(assert (= x (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (= y (fp #b1 #b00101101000 #x422c9af77f44c)))
(assert (= r (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(assert (= (fp.max_i (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)
                     (fp #b1 #b00101101000 #x422c9af77f44c))
           (fp #b0 #b00011111000 #xb0db2ac57d287)))
(check-sat)
```

The `propagate-values` tactic replaces `fp.max` with `fp.max_i` but the `simplify` tactic does not constant fold `fp.max_i`.

I might try and tackle this missing constant folding myself because this looks relatively easy to fix.

issue_body:
Here is a small system that triggers a bad_ineq_inference exception from within the interpolator. (It works fine if you use Ints instead.)
```
(set-option :fixedpoint.engine "duality")

(declare-rel Goal ())

(declare-rel B (Real Real Real))

              (= y z))
          (A x y z)))

          (B x y z)))

               (= y x)
               (> y 0)

          Goal))

(query Goal)
```

issue_title: Underflow checking of signed multiplication
issue_body:
@NikolajBjorner This is not strictly about Z3, but about the following paper:

   https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/z3prefix.pdf

(I believe some of the code in Z3 regarding overflow-checking is based on this paper, so perhaps it's related in that way.)

On page 12 when it talks about checking underflow for signed multiplication, I think we need to make an explicit check that neither `x` nor `y` equals `0`.

For instance, if `x` is `0`, and `y` is any negative number, then the hypothesis that the signs are different holds (essentially `0` being identified as sign-bit `0`), but the consequent fails since the extended multiplication produces `0`; and thus the check that all the initial `N+1` bits must be `1` fails. So, a straightforward reading of the formula will  incorrectly classify this case as an underflow.

A minor point perhaps, but wanted to double check with you that this is indeed needed. Or is there some other language in the paper that I missed that already covers this case?

issue_title: Unexpected output when "produce-proofs" is given
issue_body:
I'm playing with proof-generation features in Z3. For this input:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun s0 () Int)
(declare-fun s1 () (_ FloatingPoint  8 24))
(assert (>= s0 0))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
```

Z3 happily produces:

```
sat
((s0 0))
```

Note that I'm using `QF_FP` for the logic, since I want to mix Integers and Floats. (Is there a logic that "officially" combines them? I couldn't find it.)

However, if I insert `(set-option :produce-proofs true)` at the very top, keeping everything else the same:

```
(set-option :produce-proofs true)
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun s0 () Int)
(declare-fun s1 () (_ FloatingPoint  8 24))
(assert (>= s0 0))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
```

I get:

```
sat
((s0 Int!val!0))
```

So, it appears the `(set-option :produce-proofs true)` makes z3 decide to interpret `Int` as an uninterpreted sort in this case.

I think the setting of `QF_FP` can justify that decision, but why doesn't that happen in the original input? Or conversely, if the original is OK, why does setting `:produce-proofs` to `true` cause a different behavior here?

I'm not suggesting this is a bug, but it seems inconsistent.

issue_title: Public continuous integration testing
issue_body:
I'd like propose that publicly visible continuous integration (CI) testing is added by using [TravisCI](https://travis-ci.org/). This **does not** replace the internal testing already done. Instead its purpose is to provide early feedback by checking Z3 (and its bindings) will compile and can be installed.

The neat things about TravisCI is that it can be set up to build every pull request in addition to the current ``master`` branch. This is very handy because you can quickly see if outside contributions break the build. It will also send e-mails to the authors of commits that break the build.

TravisCI runs on Ubuntu Linux so this CI would only be for this platform. It is a free service for open source projects.

If this idea was accepted I would like to test the following configurations

* CXX=clang-3.7 Release build
* CXX=gcc-4.8 Release build
* CXX=clang-3.7 Debug build
* CXX=gcc-4.8 Debug build

We could test more configurations (e.g. different python versions) but the more configurations we add the longer it will take to complete the builds.

Each configuration would build Z3 and all the language bindings and check that install works correctly. Lightweight testing code be done on the Z3 binary in the future if desired.

I have quite a bit of experience setting up TravisCI so I'm happy to do the work here but I won't start unless I get the go-ahead from @NikolajBjorner and @wintersteiger because it would be a waste of my time to implement something that will never be accepted.

issue_title: Enhance group_barh and hist
issue_body:
I propose that we add a few additional options to these methods:

`group_barh`:
- [ ] Add a `proportions=True` optional argument.  If `True`, proportions rather than counts are plotted.
- [ ] Add an option to sort the bars in descending order by size.

`hist`:
- [ ] Add a `areas_show_counts=True` optional argument.  If `True`, counts rather than proportions are plotted.  That is, the area of each bar is proportional to the count of things in the bin.  (I don't mean that the _heights_ should be proportional to the counts, which is what `normed=False` does; that of course is silly and probably shouldn't be an option at all.)  This is a valid histogram of the counting measure on the dataset, exactly analogous to a bar chart that shows counts rather than proportions.

The goal is to make the functionality of `group_barh` and `hist` parallel, except that one plots categorical distributions and the other plots numerical ones.  I think this will simplify the introduction of bar charts and histograms in Data 8 and Data 8R by solving two problems:
1. Currently it's necessary to teach `group` before bar charts, and I think the flow could be improved if we left `group` for slightly later.  (Currently we need to use `group` to produce a table that can be sorted or augmented with proportions.)
2. The similarity between bar charts and histograms is obscured by the fact that bar charts currently show counts (without additional work to compute proportions and use `barh`) and histograms use proportions (with no way to get counts, as far as I know).

issue_title: Launch stability issues
issue_body:
As mentioned in https://github.com/electron/electron/issues/7037, we've got ourselves a persnickety problem on OS X (whose solution can affect other platforms).

We're running into an issue where command line parameter behavior is different when packaged vs. run in dev mode. When running locally in dev mode, arguments come the standard argv way which means we handle them in the ready event.

When packaged, arguments end up going to the open-file event (cool!), yet we still end up with the ready event being fired and processing the argv. Our default mode is to open a fresh new file. At this point, we don't know if the open-file event is going to happen later as a result of running it from the command line.

issue_title: size argument of get_pseudo_addr in angr 7 disappeared
issue_body:
Hi, @rhelmot 
In `angr 6`, `get_pseudo_addr` has an `size` argument that I can determine the memory size of my external object.
```
    def get_pseudo_addr(self, ident, size=16):
        if ident not in self._lookup_table:
            self._lookup_table[ident] = self._next_addr
            self._next_addr += size + ((self._granularity - size) % self._granularity)
        return self._lookup_table[ident] + self.rebase_addr
```
`angr 7` discards the `size` argument and replaces it with `alignment` argument.
So I cannot allocate custom memory size for my external object.

issue_title: Question: regarding async-mode
issue_body:
Hi Team,

I am using the async-mode to execute a Junos software package add RPC.  This is a long running process, and during the install the Junos response RPC will emit output, which is actually captured within an <output> tag.  I have code written using the async mode AOK; but I am wondering if I can access the "ncclient read buffer" while the RPC is running so that I can get "near real time" progress.  While I know that the buffer string is "incomplete" I could at least use the contents in an intelligent want to provide useful feedback on progress to the User.  

So my code effectively looks like this:

````python
    rpc_rsp = ncc.rpc(rpc_cmd)

    # check the status of the RPC against a countdown timeout ...
    while not rpc_rsp.event.is_set():
        cur_act.details['countdown'] = count_down
        cur_act.updated()
        count_down -= check_after
        if count_down < 0:
            break
        time.sleep(check_after)
````

What I'd like to do is access a property within the `rpc_rsp` object so that I can obtain the read buffer within the while loop.

I hope this question makes sense.  I look forward to your reply and guidance.

Thank you!
-- Jeremy

(ps - I am also back at Juniper)

issue_title: Add support to Npcap native mode
issue_body:
Hi, I'm the author of [Npcap](https://github.com/nmap/npcap). Thanks for using Npcap first!

I found that the current pypcap doesn't support the Npcap native mode (Npcap DLLs will be installed in ``System32\Npcap`` instead of ``System32``).  The impact is that the user has to install Npcap with ``WinPcap Compatible Mode`` option checked to use (aka ``winpcap_mode=yes``). Otherwise, pypcap cannot find Npcap DLLs. And enabling this option will install another copy of Npcap driver that is compatible with WinPcap (like installing the DLLs to ``System32``), which may affect the performance. **So Npcap suggests all its downstream software to support Npcap native mode.** Now software like Wireshark, Nmap, GNS3 has already supported Npcap native mode.

Please refer to the docs [here](https://rawgit.com/nmap/npcap/master/docs/npcap-guide-wrapper.html#npcap-feature-native) for details and implementation guidelines. An Python example can be found [here](https://github.com/secdev/scapy/pull/524#pullrequestreview-23763661).

issue_title: [history] State of objects at particular timestamp
issue_body:
There is a need to be able to see the state of an object (or list of objects) at a particular timestamp (when the history plugin is enabled).

This would consist in applying the history entry backwards to obtain the same response (+headers) as it used to be at that timestamp. The endpoint would be readonly.

There are some specific aspects that may be difficult to handle like permissions (is that user allowed to see those old records?).

An alternative approach is to do on the client side using the existing history endpoint.

See also #826

issue_title: Cannot retrieve more than `storage_max_fetch_size` records through pagination
issue_body:
It seems that the 10000 server limit is enforced on the number of records that have been queried, and not the number of records returned.

Eg: using `_limit=5000` will allow two requests to be made and "succeed" (with proper content and `Next-Page` headers). Using `_limit=100` will allow 100 requests. But using `_limit=10000` will only allow one.

issue_title: Metadata on Groups
issue_body:
Similarily to how you can store extra properties (metadata) on a collection, it would be useful to be able to do this with groups.

In my applications, almost everything is dynamic. Users can create groups on the fly, rename them, etc., so I tend to use generated ID's for everything. It would be nice to be able to set a title and description on groups for UI presentation.

Right now I have to create a collection for storing group metadata separately from the actual group.

issue_title: Request fingerprint takes all headers and additional cookies into consideration
issue_body:
Apologies if this is stated anywhere in the documentation.

From what I can see when a request object gets a chance to be run through `splash_request_fingerprint` it will have `splash.args.headers` and `splash.args.cookies` set on its meta by splash middlewares. And that makes dupefilter and cache facilities see different fingerprints for requests that kinda sorta should have been seen as identical.

I was considering moving middleware priorities around but this sorta changes for me is insanely hard to debug / test or even to think about. For now as a solution I rewrote splash dupefilter and splash cachestorage to use a custom fingerprint generator which:
* Removes `cookies` and `headers` from meta.splash.args.
* Removes any key which starts with a _ on meta.splash.
* Removes request body before calling vanilla fingerprint since all the stuff is also in there (I mostly use `/execute` endpoint).

Basic test project I checked this on: [testing.zip](https://github.com/scrapy-plugins/scrapy-splash/files/2029946/testing.zip)
Code for my workaround: https://gist.github.com/Unknowny/0cbd4967c35c3959a76ba37edb99140a

issue_title: Improve unit test coverage percentage
issue_body: Pulp Smash is being converted from a test suite to a framework for writing tests. As a result, breaking changes in the code are far more problematic. Unit tests are perfect for detecting interface changes. Let's raise unit test coverage to at least 90%.

issue_title: Travis and AppVeyor may fail at different times
issue_body:
If the standalone binary build fails during Travis CI processing, it will fail in the "deploy" job/stage but in the `script` step. If the standalone binary build fails during AppVeyor processing, it will fail in the `before_deploy` step (which only occurs if deployment conditions have been met). 

Both should fail prior to deployment because deployment does not always occur. However, if the standalone binary does not properly build, the build should fail even if deployment is not going to happen. While the standalone binary isn't strictly needed unless deployment will take place, building the standalone binary is a valuable test and let's us know as early in the process as possible that there are problems and that deployment won't be possible later without fixes.

In addition, for maintainability, keeping Travis and AppVeyor as logically close in functionality as possible is helpful.

issue_title: Support for SAM template transformations
issue_body:
As recently decribed in https://aws.amazon.com/de/about-aws/whats-new/2016/11/aws-cloudformation-supports-aws-serverless-application-model-aws-lambda-environment-variables-and-new-cli-commands CloudFormation introduces some kind of template mechanism for serverless apps called SAM (serverless application model). The idea is to have pseudo resources like "AWS::Serverless::Function" in a template which simplify the definition of lambda functions by abstracting complicated configuration. A new "Transform" top level parameter inside the template defines how CloudFormation has to transform these pseudo resources into regular ones. The mechanism is described here: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/deploying-lambda-apps.html

Cfn-Sphere should support the new "Transform" template parameter.

issue_title: pip 10 api breakage
issue_body:
Quoting distutils-sig:

> We're in the process of starting to plan for a release of pip (the
long-awaited pip 10). We're likely still a month or two away from a
release, but now is the time for people to start ensuring that
everything works for them. One key change in the new version will be
that all of the internal APIs of pip will no longer be available, so
any code that currently calls functions in the "pip" namespace will
break. Calling pip's internal APIs has never been supported, and
always carried a risk of such breakage, so projects doing so should,
in theory, be prepared for such things. However, reality is not always
that simple, and we are aware that people will need time to deal with
the implications.

> Just in case it's not clear, simply finding where the internal APIs
have moved to and calling them under the new names is *not* what
people should do. We can't stop people calling the internal APIs,
obviously, but the idea of this change is to give people the incentive
to find a supported approach, not just to annoy people who are doing
things we don't want them to ;-)

> So please - if you're calling pip's internals in your code, take the
opportunity *now* to check out the in-development version of pip, and
ensure your project will still work when pip 10 is released.

> And many thanks to anyone else who helps by testing out the new
version, as well :-)

> Thanks,
Paul
_________

Safety uses `pip.get_installed_distributions` which has moved to https://github.com/pypa/pip/blob/master/src/pip/_internal/utils/misc.py#L333

During OpenThread code analysis I've stumbled into few bits of code which generated some questions. I'm opening this issue as an umbrella one to host them all, to avoid issue spamming with single sentence stuff. If it will be necessary, I'll split separate issues and/or PRs.

Here we go:

- there are two `RouteTlv` structures, inheriting over two different bases, apart from it they are pretty identical. But one has `uint8_t mRouteData[kMaxRouterId + 1]` and `uint8_t mRouterIdMask[BitVectorBytes(kMaxRouterId + 1)]` and the other is missing `+1`. Is that correct?

- this `if` statement seems to be always true: https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/3eb552a9b7ee7a0dae65e61b4fa6d6b631daa4a4/src/core/thread/router_table.cpp#L537 and therefore can be simplified.

- `https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/12237d07dc813f72a16d4d6d4681e75680dd7569/src/core/thread/network_diagnostic_tlvs.hpp#L630` and `https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/51c62a29d0ce170e8c85e65f9f8deade0d7c575e/src/core/thread/mle_tlvs.hpp#L547` seem to be unused. issue_title: Passing sent messages back to upper layers.
issue_body:
Currently, when a higher layer component submits a message for transmission, it hands custody off to the lower layers and never gets the message back.  In cases where the higher layer wants to retransmit the same message, it is useful for the lower layer to pass custody back to upper layers.  Both MPL and reliable CoAP transmissions are two examples.

One obvious possibility is for the message header to carry a callback and context that the lower layer could use to pass the message back to the higher layer.  However, this would mean adding two additional pointers to the message header.

Thoughts?

https://github.com/python-xlib/python-xlib/blob/63d5cf15901ec945ac45c6a58b58d12b491e5a9b/Xlib/protocol/rq.py#L427

This line caused a decoding error when run with Python 3.6:

>   UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xd4 in position 0: invalid continuation byte

I'm wondering whether `data.decode()` will ever work for values in range [128, 256).

`data.decode('latin1')` solves my issue. But I think there may be similar cases in other parts of the project.

Pull Request: #85 

The default encoding in Python 3 is `utf-8`, which doesn't work in this case. `ascii` won't work, either.

issue_title: The qr() method must whitespace-pad above and below the QR code
issue_body:
```
The white border around a QR code is a non-optional part of the registration 
marking system used by scanners to recognize the code.

In order to get the libdecodeqr-simpletest example program to recognize the QR 
code printed by the example code at 
https://code.google.com/p/python-escpos/wiki/Usage and decode it, I had to:

1. Scan it
2. Open it in GIMP
3. Use the eraser tool to replace part of the logo.gif contents above and 
barcode below with white.

While I understand taking the full 13 digits of an EAN13 barcode as input since 
barcode readers and ISBN-13 databases treat the checksum digit as part of the 
data, there is no similar reason to let users generate broken QR codes.
```

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `stephan....@gmail.com` on 20 May 2014 at 1:59

issue_title: Unreachable code
issue_body:
There are a number of if statements where code similar to the following is executed:

```
if not self.connectEvent.wait():
    self.logger.warning("Unable ... not currently connected")
    return False
```

Those log messages can never be executed and False can never be returned.  The threading.Event wait() method always returns True unless a timeout value is given and the wait times out.  Since there is no timeout, the call will always block.
* [Python2](https://docs.python.org/2/library/threading.html#event-objects)
* [Python3](https://docs.python.org/3/library/threading.html#event-objects)

Should the wait calls be modified to something like so that the connectEvent flag is checked:

```
# The number of seconds to block for the connectEvent event to be set.
CONNECT_WAIT_SEC = 0.001

if not self.connectEvent.wait(CONNECT_WAIT_SEC):
    self.logger.warning("Unable ... not currently connected")
    return False
```

issue_title: Confusing failure in remote mode for GAPIC config pipeline
issue_body:
### Expected behavior

If a GAPIC config already exists and the pipeline is running in remote mode, either
- artman fails and returns a meaningful error message, or
- artman overwrites the config in the archive that is downloaded
### Observed behavior

Artman fails with no information. Logs indicate only that the config was generated successfully, since the failure occurs at the copying stage -- after the config is actually generated.
### Context

Artman does not automatically overwrite existing configs because of the risk of overwriting uncommitted manual edits, which occur frequently in the development process. However, this risk exists only when running Artman in local mode, since in remote mode, nothing is ever overwritten on the local machine. We can therefore safely overwrite.

If detecting the environment (local/remote) is complicated, a clear error message is a good short-term solution.

issue_title: Fast way to load multiple models from files?
issue_body:
I'm building a system for classifying images into multiple categories, and approaching the problem using multiple networks (all my networks have the same structure, and output `[0,1]` or `[1,0]` (Yes or No).

There's (for the sake of the example) a network for dogs, another network for cats, the third network is for cars, and so and so...

**My first question**: Is this kind of approach the popular one, or is there a different (and hopefully better) approach for classifying a LOT of different subjects?

If I'm on the right path, I wondered if there a fast way to load the models from their files using TFLearn.

Currently, I have a function to define my model structure:

    def define_network_model(category_id):
        """
        Defines a network structure
        """`
    
        # Defining image augmentation
        # We want to create a stilted wider dataset
        aug = ImageAugmentation()
        aug.add_random_flip_leftright()
        aug.add_random_blur(sigma_max=3.)
        aug.add_random_rotation(max_angle=20.)
        
        T.reset_default_graph()
    
        # Input layer
        net = input_data(shape=[None, IMG_SIZE, IMG_SIZE, 1], name="input", \
            data_augmentation=aug)
    
        # 2D Convolution layer
        net = conv_2d(net, 32, 5, activation='relu')
        net = max_pool_2d(net, 3)
    
        .... More layers ....
    
        # Output layer
        net = fully_connected(net, 2, activation='softmax')
    
        # Regression layer
        net = regression(net, optimizer='adam', learning_rate=LEARNING_RATE, \
            loss='categorical_crossentropy', name='targets')
        
        # Creating a model
        model = tflearn.DNN(net, tensorboard_dir="tensorboard", \
            best_checkpoint_path=os.path.join(CHECKPOINTS_DIR, category_id), \
            best_val_accuracy=0.7)
    
        return model

And when I want to load the models, I have a categories object I'm iterating through and loading all the categories:

    models = {}
    
    for id, cat in categories.items():
        m = define_network_model(id) # Creating the structure
        m.load(os.path.join(MODELS_DIR, id)) # Loading the file
        models[id] = m # Appending to the models list for later evaluation


Defining the network every iteration takes **a LOT** of time (>2sec for every iteration).

**My second question**: Am I missing something here? Can the structure be cloned from a model to another in a faster way, or am I just on the wrong path?

issue_title: scipy_interfaces.fftpack needs _next_regular to support upstream changes
issue_body:
`_next_regular` is designed to support fft implementation specific optimisations. In the case of FFTW, this is different to fftpack, so a custom one should be included (it is used by scipy internal tools).

See https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/6124.

issue_title: Driver compatible to version 0.10.x of BigchainDB should have version greater 0.2.x
issue_body:
Our repo here has a dependency on bigchaindb-driver and bigchaindb as follows: https://github.com/bigchaindb/pycoalaip-bigchaindb/blob/master/setup.py#L14

```
install_requires = [
    'coalaip==0.0.1',
    'bigchaindb_driver~=0.2.0',
]

tests_require = [
    'tox>=2.3.1',
    'coverage>=4.1',
    'flake8>=2.6.0',
    'pytest>=3.0.1',
    'pytest-cov',
    'pytest-mock',
    'bigchaindb~=0.9.0',
]
```

`~` only upgrades the PATCH version of a package: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH.

In this configuration what pip will do is:

- install bigchaindb-driver 0.2.2
- install bigchaindb 0.9.5

However, bigchaindb-driver 0.2.2 requires at least bigchaindb 0.10.1 meaning pip will error out with:

```
(venv) linkeex@Tims-MacBook-Pro-3  ~/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0   master ●  omi-api
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 612, in _build_master
    ws.require(__requires__)
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 918, in require
    needed = self.resolve(parse_requirements(requirements))
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 810, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist, req).with_context(dependent_req)
pkg_resources.ContextualVersionConflict: (BigchainDB 0.9.5 (/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages), Requirement.parse('bigchaindb~=0.10.1'), {'bigchaindb-driver'})

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/bin/omi-api", line 5, in <module>
    from pkg_resources import load_entry_point
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 3018, in <module>
    working_set = WorkingSet._build_master()
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 614, in _build_master
    return cls._build_from_requirements(__requires__)
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 627, in _build_from_requirements
    dists = ws.resolve(reqs, Environment())
  File "/Users/linkeex/Projects/omi-mvi-1.0/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 805, in resolve
    raise DistributionNotFound(req)
pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: bigchaindb~=0.10.1
```

I can see two options that could work:

Upgrade bigchaindb-driver's MINOR version always with bigchaindb's minor version (then tilde doesn't fuck things up), or in setup.py do not require bigchaindb > 0.9.5 (which is probably not possible as internal interfaces have been broken (which is OK).

Hence, bigchaindb-driver 0.2.2 should actually be 0.3.0.

@sbellem hope that makes sense :D

Tip: Pipe multiple files into csvjoin
issue_body:
Hey guys, I noticed in [the docs](http://csvkit.readthedocs.org/en/540/tricks.html#specifying-stdin-as-a-file) a tip about piping a file to commands like `csvjoin`. In my case, I wanted to pipe the results of multiple streams into `csvjoin` for a [data workflow](https://github.com/CityOfPhiladelphia/property-assessments-data-pipeline), so I did some digging and came across [named pipes](http://www.linuxjournal.com/article/2156). For example, if you want to cut 2 files before joining them:

```bash
csvjoin -c "id,id" <(csvcut -c 2,5,6 file_a.csv) <(csvcut -c 1,7 file_b.csv)
```
Thought it might be handy to mention in the tricks section if you're interested.

issue_title: segfault mystery
get_include() does not return the location of the header files
issue_body:
For comparison, in numpy get_include returns the location of the numpy header files:

```
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.get_include()
'/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/numpy/core/include'
```

However, doing the same with pybind11 one gets

```
>>> import pybind11 as py
>>> py.get_include()
'/usr/local/include/python3.5m'
```

and there are no pybind11 header files in that location.

I have installed `pybind11` by doing

```
$ python setup.py install 
```
from its source folder.

issue_title: scoped_ostream_redirect only works for the first 1024 characters
Keyword arguments mismatch with C++ function arguments
issue_body:
I have a C++ function with the following signature:
```C++
spv_float well_path_ident(
	ulong nx, ulong ny, spv_float coord, spv_float zcorn,
	spv_float well_info, bool include_well_nodes, const char* strat_traits = "online_tops",
	const ulong min_split_threshold = 0, spv_ulong hit_idx = nullptr
);
```
Here `ulong` is `std::size_t`, and `spv_float, spv_ulong` are `std::shared_ptr` pointers to my custom array type which is inherited from `pybind11::array_t`. I've written custom converters for it that are very similar to `Eigen` binding code. Converters are working as expected in my tests.

Next, I export the function above with the following snippet:
```C++
m.def("well_path_ident", &well_path_ident,
	"nx"_a, "ny"_a, "coord"_a, "zcorn"_a, "well_info"_a, "include_well_nodes"_a = true,
	"mesh_traits"_a = "online_tops", "min_split_threshold"_a = 0, "hit_idx"_a = nullptr
);
```
Everything compiles just fine! `pybind11` correctly counts arguments of `well_path_ident` -- if I make a mistake (for ex. forget one argument) it will notify me with an error at compile time.

But after module is loaded in Python here's what I get with `help(my_module)` command:
```
well_path_ident(...)
        well_path_ident(*args, **kwargs)
        Overloaded function.

        1. well_path_ident(nx: int, ny: int, numpy.ndarray[float64], numpy.ndarray[float64], numpy.ndarray[float64], coord: bool, zcorn: unicode, well_info: int, numpy.ndarray[uint64]) -> numpy.ndarray[float64]
```
(I have one more overload of this function, but it has the same issue).

As you can see, keyword arguments "skip" `numpy.ndarray[float64]` (spv_float) for some reason, and starting from "coord" they bind to wrong C++ function arguments.

I feel that this is related in some way to custom type conversion code, but what really can cause such issue?
Btw, when I try to call this function from Python, I get an exception:
```
RuntimeError: Unknown internal error occurred
```
And actual C++ function isn't called, I can't get into it in debugger.

issue_title: Broken mro when using multiple inheritance with py::metaclass
issue_body:
When a derived class `MroDerived` needs to expose a static attribute (which necessitates using `py::metaclass`), but also inherits from multiple bases Python raises the following error:

`ImportError: MroDerived: PyType_Ready failed (TypeError: mro() returned base with unsuitable layout ('pybind11_tests.MroBaseB'))!`

For a complete example see https://github.com/lsst-dm/pybind11/tree/broken_mro

issue_title: Django Admin Search FILTER_ERROR
issue_body:
When using django-ldapdb and using the search form in the admin page, a FILTER_ERROR occurs.

Search Fields defined in admin.py: 

```
class LdapGroupAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    exclude = ['dn', 'password', 'photo']
    list_display = ['cn', 'gidNumber', 'mumberUid']
    search_fields = ['gidNumber',]

admin.site.register(models.LdapGroup, LdapGroupAdmin)
```

 The following error occurs: 

```
FILTER_ERROR at /ldapdb/admin/ldapapi/ldapgroup/
{'desc': u'Bad search filter'}
```

 The search filter being sent to pyldap appears to be:

```
'(&(objectClass=top)(objectClass=posixGroup)(ldapapi_ldapgroup.gidNumber LIKE %959959%))',
```

issue_title: Support PetSet
issue_body:
Now we are using `PetSet` to deploy our cluster applications. We accept request in `Django` and it would be better to access `Kubernetes` with Python SDK like `pykube`.

After reading https://github.com/kelproject/pykube/blob/99e193a81f59044e1d6f3a3b96e6e33853059749/pykube/__init__.py , I thinks `pykube` doesn't support `PetSet` yet. Is it possible to support `PetSet`? 

We're also glad to test it.

issue_title: Allow requesting wildcard certificates in the client
issue_body:
The ACME spec allows for the possibility of wildcard certificates. Certbot is an ACME client that should be able to be used by any ACME server so, as suggested by @terribleplan, we should try to allow users to request wildcard certificates as well.

There's a number of things to consider when doing this, however. First, since Let's Encrypt is currently the only ACME server, all requests for wildcard certificates will fail. With this in mind, we should make sure that the error message given to the user when they request wildcard certificates is reasonable. Ideally, this is done by showing the error message returned by Let's Encrypt. If that doesn't work, we can either add our own warning message or own error checking when the server is Let's Encrypt.

issue_title: certbot error path for help gives misleading help
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

debian 9.0

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

certbot-auto

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

```
$ certbot-auto renew --help
```
```
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  certbot-auto [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [-d DOMAIN] [-d DOMAIN] ...

Certbot can obtain and install HTTPS/TLS/SSL certificates.  By default,
it will attempt to use a webserver both for obtaining and installing the
certificate. The most common SUBCOMMANDS and flags are:

obtain, install, and renew certificates:
    (default) run   Obtain & install a certificate in your current webserver
    certonly        Obtain or renew a certificate, but do not install it
    renew           Renew all previously obtained certificates that are near
expiry
   -d DOMAINS       Comma-separated list of domains to obtain a certificate for

  --apache          Use the Apache plugin for authentication & installation
  --standalone      Run a standalone webserver for authentication
  --nginx           Use the Nginx plugin for authentication & installation
  --webroot         Place files in a server's webroot folder for authentication
  --manual          Obtain certificates interactively, or using shell script
hooks

   -n               Run non-interactively
  --test-cert       Obtain a test certificate from a staging server
  --dry-run         Test "renew" or "certonly" without saving any certificates
to disk

manage certificates:
    certificates    Display information about certificates you have from Certbot
    revoke          Revoke a certificate (supply --cert-path)
    delete          Delete a certificate

manage your account with Let's Encrypt:
    register        Create a Let's Encrypt ACME account
  --agree-tos       Agree to the ACME server's Subscriber Agreement
   -m EMAIL         Email address for important account notifications

More detailed help:

  -h, --help [TOPIC]    print this message, or detailed help on a topic;
                        the available TOPICS are:

   all, automation, commands, paths, security, testing, or any of the
   subcommands or plugins (certonly, renew, install, register, nginx,
   apache, standalone, webroot, etc.)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

The syntax is listed at the top as:
```
  certbot-auto [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [-d DOMAIN] [-d DOMAIN] ...
```

It's true that SUBCOMMAND is optional, and thus one could almost make the claim that the syntax isn't wrong, but in practice, it's really quite misleading.

One wouldn't run `certbot-auto -h -d ...`, and while one could try to run `certbot-auto -h renew -d example.com`, it wouldn't do anything useful (beyond running `certbot-auto -h renew`).

I half expect to see:
```
  certbot-auto [-h|--help] [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [-d DOMAIN] [-d DOMAIN] ...
```
Or 
```
  certbot-auto [certbotoptions] [SUBCOMMAND] [subcommandoptions] [-d DOMAIN] [-d DOMAIN] ...
```

Reading the `certbot-auto` script itself indicates that there are at least the following certbotoptions:
```

  --debug                                   attempt experimental installation
  -h, --help                                print this help
  -n, --non-interactive, --noninteractive   run without asking for user input
  --no-bootstrap                            do not install OS dependencies
  --no-self-upgrade                         do not download updates
  --os-packages-only                        install OS dependencies and exit
  -v, --verbose                             provide more output
  -q, --quiet                               provide only update/error output;
                                            implies --non-interactive
```

Notably missing from the help:
* --debug
* --quiet
* --verbose

Also, `The most common SUBCOMMANDS and flags are:` is misleading, as it implies that `-h` is a subcommand flag, when in fact it's very much not.

Imo, both sides of this help path can be improved:
1. it'd be really nice if `certbot subcommand -h` worked.
2. improving the syntax line.
2b. something in this page should at least hint at how to find out about the flags i'm noting that aren't listed in this help.
3. while -d is listed very prominently, it turns out you can't even do `certbot-auto renew -d example.com`, which means sticking the -d suggestion right below the renew line is really misleading:
```
    (default) run   Obtain & install a certificate in your current webserver
    certonly        Obtain or renew a certificate, but do not install it
    renew           Renew all previously obtained certificates that are near
expiry
   -d DOMAINS       Comma-separated list of domains to obtain a certificate for
```

(Personally, i'd rather renew actually supported -d, but for the time being, since it doesn't, renew should be moved below the -d help line.)

issue_title: Define a strategy to handle failed transactions because the account can't pay for the gas
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Transactions may fail because the user's account doesn't have enough ether to pay for the gas (#1815). This will result in the smart contract proxies raising an exception: after the transaction is sent, it's execution will fail, and once the proxy polls the receipt an exception is raised.

This can happen under two circumstances:

1. The smart contract proxies are used directly. This can be done when:
  - The user uses the proxies directly from the console
  - Some of the RaidenAPI's method are not interacting with the state machine, therefor the actions are not recoverable
  - Some of the REST APIs use the respective methods from RaidenAPI which are not state machine aware.
2. The proxies are used on a raiden event handler.

For the first scenario it's okay to fail with an exception, for either case a transaction or error message can be shown to the user. But, for the second scenario, the state of the raiden node will be changed, and we cant recover.

Example: The user wants to close the channel and calls `close`, this action is recoverable therefor it's written to the WAL, this is important because a channel that will be closed **must not** mediated transfers anymore (the node which is calling close cannot update the balance proof), after the state change is saved to the WAL and handled by the state machine, the `ContractSendChannelClose` event will be emitted which is responsible to send the closing transaction on-chain. If the transaction fails because the account doesn't have enough gas some action must be taken:

- The Raiden node should quit
- A thread must wait until the account has enough ether to pay for the transaction, and then the transaction must be retried (related to #483)

Alternatively, we can look for strategies to ensure that the account will always have enough ether to pay for the transaction.

issue_title: Use cell meta data to store kernel information.
issue_body:
Related to https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1883

Right now SoS has a problem in that when we execute a previous cell, it will execute it with the current active kernel, instead of the kernel that created that cell. It would be better to

1. record the kernel information of each cell
2. display cell with different color for different kernel
3. use the kernel that created the cell when re-evaluate a cell
4. the `%use` magic only applies to new cell, not existing one...

This looks difficult, or even impossible, but it seems that we can achieve this through the use of cell meta information. For example, if we look at the code at https://github.com/ipython-contrib/jupyter_contrib_nbextensions/blob/master/src/jupyter_contrib_nbextensions/nbextensions/exercise2/main.js , it is clear that the java script code has access to the underlying cell's meta data. It then should be easy to use a different color for the cell.

issue_title: Celery as external task executor
issue_body:
On the celery branch. 

step 1: start redis server

```
% redis-server
```

step 2: start celery worker

```
% celery -A pysos.sos_step worker
```

step 3: start flower monitor

```
% flower --port=5555 --broker=redis://localhost
```

and point browser to `http://localhost:5555`

step 3: execute script

```
% cd test/script
% sos run test_rq.sos
```

The flower task monitor seems to be better-looking than rq-dashboard. It appears that the celery worker creates 10 threads (tasks) so the tasks are executed in parallel, which is different from rq worker, which seems to execute tasks sequentially. 

Again, this is very preliminary, just to show how celery can be invoked to execute tasks. Although Celery is more complex than rq, it seems to be more powerful than rq and should perhaps be our choice.

issue_title: windows support
issue_body:
Tested sos under windows just because I have a windows machine at hand. It turns out that sos supports windows except for the `blassings` module, which requires the `ncurse` terminal support that is unavailable under windows.

The other problem is that the `sos` script without extension is difficult to use (require `python /path/to/sos`) so some sort of `py2exe` or `cx_Freeze` is required. Then it turns out that these two tools do not support python 3.5 well.

We perhaps also need to add action to execute windows `bat` file. We are using both `run` and `bash` for bash scripts. I suppose we should use `bash` to long bash scripts, `run` for simple list of commands that are compatible with both windows bat and linux shell. 

The bottom line is that we can make sos windows compatible without much effort and perhaps some users can find creative use of this tool under windows.

issue_title: Parameter improvements
issue_body:
Working with parameters, I have noticed there are some features missing that I think could be a useful addition. 
I have listed them below.
The suggested improvements are mainly in the StandardParameter and the CombinedParameter.
Please let me know what suggestions you agree/disagree with, and possible ways (and pitfalls!) of implementing it.

# General
## Add get/set modifier
The `val_mapping` functionality is useful, because it allows the input to be transformed before performing the actual set/get.
The limitation here is that `val_mapping` must be a dict, and an enhancement would be to also accept functions for getting/setting.
This could be especially useful for the CombinedParameter, where it would allow you to use a single value to set a linear combination of several parameters.

## Move delay to BaseParameter
Parameters other than the StandardParameter might need a delay.
It would therefore be good to standardize the `set` method to always perform a delay if the attribute is not set to None.
This could furthermore be used for `inter_delay` (see below).
I'm not sure what would be the best approach for this.
One idea would be to add it to the BaseParameter, which then decorates the set and get commands to include delays etc.
Another way is to create a method such as `_set_with_delay` or `_set_decorated_`, which is triggered by `call()`, and which then calls a `set` and performs delays.


# Parameter
## Default slice step 
Being able to use the slice notation for parameters is very useful, as it is a quick way to create an `np.arange` list.
Currently, the slice notation requires three inputs: `[start:stop:step]`.
However, both the default slice notation and np.arange don't need the `step` argument, and use the default value 1.
To mimick this behaviour, I suggest we also use 1 as a default value for `step`.

## Accept numpy arrays for sweep points
When creating sweep values for a parameter (`param[val_list]`), `val_list` must be a list.
Often one would like to use values through numpy functions, such as `arange` or `linspace`.
Currently, parameters do not accept numpy arrays for sweep values, and so these arrays must first be converted to lists.
I suggest we also allow numpy arrays as sweep values.
As a side question, would this then need to be converted to a list internally?

# StandardParameter

## Replacing methods by dependent properties
Several methods are strongly related to getting/setting attributes (e.g. get_delay, set_delay). 
These can be replaced dependent attributes (via the @property decorator). 
This results in more readible code, and less methods cluttering up the namespace.

## Useless max_delay
From looking at the code, it seems to me that `max_delay` is not doing anything.
I've done some testing, and could not come up with a situation where max_delay actually changes something.
It is also not clear to me from the documentation what its use is.
Is someone using it, or does someone understand its use? 
If not I suggest we remove it.

## Change `delay` to `post_delay`, and add `inter_delay`
Currently, the attribute `delay` results in a delay after every `set` operation.
The main use of this is to let the system settle after such a set operation.

In some cases, a different type of delay can be useful, namely to ensure that two `set` operations are not performed too rapidly after each other.
One possible reason for this is that sending visa commands too rapidly could freeze up a device.
In this case, the current `delay` results in an unnecessary delay after each set operation.
To illustrate this, assume an instrument parameter that can only process one command per second. 
After setting the parameter, an acquisition can be performed, which we assume also takes a second.
If we use the current `delay`, it will ensure that no two commands are sent within a second after each other.
However, setting a parameter and then subsequently performing an acquisition will take a total of two second instead of one.
This is because it will sleep for a second after setting the parameter, while it should be able to perform the acquisition straight away.

I suggest we change the current `delay` to `post_delay`, indicating that it will perform the delay after the set operation.
Adding a second `inter_delay` can be used to ensure that set operations are not performed too rapidly after each other.
This delay is not performed right after each set, but instead occurs when a subsequent set is performed too rapidly.
In this case, the system sleeps until `inter_delay` has passed, after which it will perform the subsequent set.

## Change `_validate_and_set` and `_validate_and_sweep` to `set`
The two methods have a lot of overlapping code.
Furthermore, adding validate to the method name seems unnecessary (the ManualParameter.set does this implicitly)
I think it would be better to combine these two methods in the set method, which starts with checking if `delay is None`


# CombinedParameter
## Make CombinedParameter an actual parameter
Instead of being a Parameter, the CombinedParameter seems to have an attribute called parameter that somehow mimicks the behaviour of a parameter.
I'm not seeing why it can't simply be a parameter, either a subclass of Parameter, MultiParameter, or BaseParameter.
The comments of @giulioungaretti  say that it's a hack, so could you explain why this hack is needed?
I suggest we transform it into a parameter.
We could then create a separate SweepValues class for the CombinedParameter.

## Default value for name
The CombinedParameter currently requires a name.
This means that when using `combine(param1, param2, ...)`, it is always necessary to also include a name.
Since the name usually refers to the parameters that are combined, we can have name as an optional argument, and have a default name.
This could be '{param1.name}_{param2.name}_...' or 'combined({param1.name}, {param2.name})' or something similar.

## Add `get` method
There is no get method for the CombinedParameter, while in many cases this would be useful.
For instance, if you have two voltage gates which you want to treat as one. 
There are several ways in which a `get` method can be implemented.
I think the best solution would be to by default return a set containing values for each of the parameters.
This default behaviour can be overridden by a get_modifier (see above).

## Add allow_scalar
One useful feature of the CombinedParameter is to treat several parameters as one.
To this end, it would be useful to be able to set multiple parameters through a single value, i.e. `combined_parameter(val)` would set each of its parameter to `val`.
We could therefore add a flag `allow_scalar`, which for safety reasons should be set to False by default

## Remove _aggregate method
We should be able to simplify this by simply using `self.aggregate = aggregator`

## Modify `set` to receive values instead of index
By doing this, we can use the CombinedParameter while not necessraily in a loop

## Add `__getitem__` notation
This probably ties in with making the CombinedParameter a parameter.
This way, we won't have to use the combined_parameter.sweep() method, which isn't necessary for other parameter types.

issue_title: Problem with current transforms.Scale implementation
issue_body:
The current [Scale implementation](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/transforms.py#L94) accepts as argument a single integer `size`, and rescales the image making `size` the new size of the smaller edge.

This results in a `RuntimeError: inconsistent tensor sizes` when the Scale transform is used on datasets containing images where the smaller edge is not always the same. 

Is transforms.Scale only intended for datasets where the image shape is consistent??

As an example, the dataset of this [Kaggle competition](https://www.kaggle.com/c/intel-mobileodt-cervical-cancer-screening) contains images of varying sizes, wich shows the problem with the current implementation:
```python
d = "/train/Type_1/"
s = Scale(300)
for i in os.listdir(d):
    img = Image.open(d+i)
    print("Original: {}".format(img.size))
    resized = s(img)
    print("Resized: {}".format(resized.size))
```
```
Original: (3096, 4128)
Resized: (300, 400)

Original: (4128, 3096)
Resized: (400, 300)
```
An raises the previously mentioned error when you try to use the Scale transform as follows:
```python
train_set = ImageFolder(train_path, 
                        transform=Compose([Scale(300),
                                           ToTensor()
                                          ]))
train_loader = DataLoader(train_set, 
                          batch_size=8, 
                          shuffle=True)
for i, (images, labels) in enumerate(train_loader):
    images = Variable(images)
    labels = Variable(labels)

RuntimeError: inconsistent tensor sizes at /data/users/soumith/miniconda2/conda-bld/pytorch-cuda80-0.1.10_1488757768560/work/torch/lib/TH/generic/THTensorMath.c:2548
```

Right now I'm using my own version of the Scale transform:

```python
class Reshape(object):
    """ Rescales the input PIL.Image to the given 'shape'.
    Parameters
    ----------
    shape : tuple of int
    interpolation: Default: PIL.Image.BILINEAR
    
    """

    def __init__(self, shape, interpolation=Image.BILINEAR):
        self.shape = shape
        self.interpolation = interpolation

    def __call__(self, img):
        
        return img.resize(self.shape, self.interpolation)
```

Should I pull request this as a new transform or is there an interesetstin rewriting Scale??

issue_title: Implement named actors.
issue_body:
The goal of "named actors" is to make it possible for one job to get a handle to an actor that was created by a different job (both jobs would have to be part of the same Ray cluster).

We have to decide on the semantics as well as the API. @pcmoritz proposed some possible APIs.

Assuming we have created an actor with

```python
a = ActorClass.remote()
```
### Possible API 1

```python
# Create named actor.
ray.actors["actor1"] = a

# Get named actor handle (done by a different driver).
a = ray.actors["actor1"]
```

### Possible API 2

```python
# Create named actor.
ray.register_actor("actor1", a)

# Get named actor handle (done by a different driver).
a = ray.get_actor("actor1")
```

### Possible API 3

Another approach, suggested by @stephanie-wang is to just create a single global actor (global to the cluster), which each driver has a handle to, and which the user can use to implement named actors if they want to.

```python
```

I have attached the source code with the input file which can be run directly.
Here is the code:

`import csv
import sys
import ray

ray.init()
# --------------------------------------------------------------- #
# A function to read the csv file, & store the data in the form:  #
# columns id|timestamp|[identifier1,identifier2,...]              #
# --------------------------------------------------------------- #
# ---------------------- Start of Function ---------------------- #
@ray.remote
def extractdata(file):
    List_of_Data = [] # A list to save all the csv data in it
    f = open(file, 'r', encoding="utf8")
    file_read = csv.reader(f, delimiter=';', quoting=csv.QUOTE_NONE)
    for row in file_read:

def writeCSV(list_to_write, OutputFileName):
    file = open(OutputFileName, 'w', newline='')
    writer = csv.writer(file, quotechar='"', delimiter=';',quoting=csv.QUOTE_ALL, skipinitialspace=True)
    for row in list_to_write:

def data_to_nodes(list_of_all_data):

            sublist = [item[0], item[1]] # Append the node id and timestamp to sublist if the identifiers is not empty
            identifier_list = []  # This list will contain all the identifiers
            identifiers = item[2].split(",")
            for identifier in identifiers:
                identifier_list.append(identifier)
            sublist.append(identifier_list)  # Append all the identifiers to sublist as a list
            node_list.append(sublist)
    return node_list
# ----------------------- End of Function ----------------------- #

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
# Define a function to convert the nodes list to csv desired format  #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
# ---------------------- Start of Function ---------------------- #
@ray.remote
def nodes_to_formatted_nodes(list_of_nodes):
    formatted_nodes = []
    for item in list_of_nodes:
        sublist = []
        sublist.append(str(item[0]))
        sublist.append(item[1])
        sublist.append(','.join(map(str, item[2])))
        formatted_nodes.append(sublist)
    return formatted_nodes
# ----------------------- End of Function ----------------------- #
#################################################################################################################################################
###############################################################################
#                                                                             #
#                    The Main Part of the Program Starts Here                 #
#                                                                             #
###############################################################################

# Input and output file names
InFile = "examples/wikipedia.csv"
#InFile = "examples/prep-e2g.csv"
# InFile = sys.argv[1]
InFileName = InFile.split('\\').pop().split('/').pop().rsplit('.', 1)[0]

AllData = extractdata.remote(InFile)

# =========================================================================== #
#          Creation of nodes by calling the function defined above            #
# =========================================================================== #
nodes = data_to_nodes.remote(AllData)

# Formatting the nodes to make suitable for writing to desired csv file
nodes_formatted = nodes_to_formatted_nodes.remote(nodes)

# Write nodes to a csv file
writeCSV.remote(nodes_formatted,'output_files/'+InFileName+'-nodes.csv')`

It can be a little frustrating if a user has an html email footer and a client which doesn't send a plain text version of the message. All messages are hidden and the conversation flow in the web interface is feels disjointed. It would be brilliant if the text content within the html could be grabbed and displayed. I understand the potential complications of parsing html (especially malformed) but I think it could add a lot of value.

The alternative approach being embedding documents in the conversation feed itself... feels a bit weird. Although it does hand the work and complications off to the browser which may be worth it.

Thanks for all your work on this app!

So if we have the following code:

```python
@app.route('/menu', methods=['POST'])
def menu():

    command = 'echo ' + param + ' >> ' + 'menu.txt'
    hey = 'echo ' + param + ' >> ' + 'menu.txt'

```

issue_title: Potentially ballooning notebook size if repeated widget state save
issue_body:
Every time you run a notebook with widgets, if you save widgets state, it will add up to all previous states, resulting in a potentially ballooning notebook size.  

While this is clear why it is the case a beginner or intermediate or scatterbrain user (like myself !) might end up with gigantic notebooks, and mistakenly commit them or convert them to html before removing unnecessary parts.   

To avoid that it may be more user friendly to have a default 'clear all previous state and save current widget state' behavior.  

I think of several possibilities.  
1. The regular save button does 'clear all widget states and save widget states and notebook'
2. A new option below 'Save and Checkpoint' named 'Save incl. widget state' does 1.
3. The top button in the Widgets menu is like 2.

Additionally in the Widgets menu, it would be useful to have convenience options like
+ Clear widget state & output
+ Clear widget state and restart kernel and run all

In my experience they would be very frequently used in prototyping mode.

Food for thought.  
None of the above is critical, but convenient and would ease the readability and adoption of widgets.
(Sometimes tiny details are cumbersome obstacles - remember the enable command)

issue_title: Missing support for "forced tracing"
issue_body:
I think you use the trace options from StackDriver incorrectly, based on this:
https://cloud.google.com/trace/docs/support?hl=sr#how_do_i_force_a_request_to_be_traced

`o=1` should mean forced tracing, regardless of the decision of the sampler. So here:

https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/cfcfda584ba984e6f0e89684120c1ea94271b2c9/opencensus/trace/tracer.py#L68-L77

you should probably say `or` instead of `and` but please correct me if I'm missing something. To me it's more intuitive from a developer POV to have a way to force tracing for debugging reasons when needed and otherwise use some constant sampling rate. And maybe I would rename `enabled` to `forced`. I'm happy to open PR ofc if this makes sense by first look to you

issue_title: Document supported Python versions in README, and PyPi
issue_body:
When I am considering using a new Python library, perhaps the most important information I need is to be able to tell what versions of Python the library supports.

Typically I look for:

1. I look for PyPi badges at the top of the README.MD file. ([for an example, see pyhocon](https://github.com/chimpler/pyhocon))

issue_title: Contributing guidelines
issue_body:
I think it is time for us to discuss the creation of contributing guidelines. Benefit would be that it helps others who want to contribute to this project but also as a documentation for the rest of us. I don't think we need to go all out on this like [pandas](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/contributing.html) or [numpy](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-dev/dev/index.html) but rather keep it relatively short, like [rasterio](https://github.com/mapbox/rasterio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.rst).

What points should be covered?

- [ ] design principles / code style guide
- [ ] developing environment / How to create a PR for a fix or feature?
- [ ] testing / writing tests (Travis and local)
- [ ] documentation and changelog
- [ ] release and packaging
